title: Fix secretsdb cache consistency and improve memory efficiency
body:
# Fix secretsdb cache consistency and improve memory efficiency
## Summary

This PR simplifies the secrets database schema by removing the redundant `IDToName` map and fixes a bug where the in-memory cache could become inconsistent with the on-disk database when marshal or write operations fail.

## Changes

### 1. Fix cache/file consistency on errors

**Bug:** When `store()` or `delete()` modified the in-memory cache and then failed during `json.MarshalIndent()` or `os.WriteFile()`, the cache would be left in a modified state while the file retained the old data — causing inconsistency.

**Fix:** Use `defer` with named return values to rollback cache changes on any error:

- **`store()`**: On error, deletes the newly added entries from cache
- **`delete()`**: On error, restores the previously deleted entries to cache

### 2. Remove redundant `IDToName` map

The `db` struct previously maintained three maps:
- `Secrets`: ID → Secret (contains Name)
- `NameToID`: Name → ID
- `IDToName`: ID → Name ← **redundant**

Since `Secrets[id].Name` already provides the ID→Name mapping, `IDToName` was unnecessary duplication. This PR removes it and updates all lookups to use the `Secrets` map directly.

### 3. General code improvements 
- Add newDB() constructor 
- Remove dead code
- Improve error messages (related https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/27635)

## Files Changed
- `common/pkg/secrets/secretsdb.go`
- `common/pkg/secrets/secrets.go`

## Testing

- [x] Existing unit tests pass
- [x] Added failing test on older revision. 

## Checklist

- [x] Code follows project conventions
- [x] No new linter errors introduced
- [x] Backwards compatible (JSON schema change removes unused field)

comments.body: ✅ A new PR has been created in buildah to vendor these changes: **https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/6585**

I see tests fail, maybe because I used chmod to simulate io errors and test environment doesn't support it 🤔 ?
```
=== RUN   TestDBRollback
=== RUN   TestDBRollback/delete_rollback_on_write_failure
    secretsdb_test.go:36: 
        	Error Trace:	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/container-libs/common/pkg/secrets/secretsdb_test.go:36
        	Error:      	An error is expected but got nil.
        	Test:       	TestDBRollback/delete_rollback_on_write_failure
=== RUN   TestDBRollback/store_rollback_on_write_failure
    secretsdb_test.go:57: 
        	Error Trace:	/var/tmp/go/src/github.com/containers/container-libs/common/pkg/secrets/secretsdb_test.go:57
        	Error:      	An error is expected but got nil.
        	Test:       	TestDBRollback/store_rollback_on_write_failure
--- FAIL: TestDBRollback (0.00s)
    --- FAIL: TestDBRollback/delete_rollback_on_write_failure (0.00s)
    --- FAIL: TestDBRollback/store_rollback_on_write_failure (0.00s)
```


any idea on whats the best practice here?

1. You are right about IDToNames, I completely forgot that backward compatibility is a thing here.
2. and about `loadDB`, I missed that every operation is reloading the DB. I guess I might just close the PR already :)) 
3. There is also a bigger version of the same issue, in `SecretsManager` methods `Delete` and `Store`, where it first updates the driver and then updates the db. 
If driver succeeds and db fails, its another inconsistency.
Isn't a rename atomic? We can use a temp file and rename (atomic), if write succeeds. although true transactional consistency is unreachable it seems because of the drivers.

> There is also a bigger version of the same issue, in `SecretsManager` methods `Delete` and `Store`, where it first updates the driver and then updates the db. If driver succeeds and db fails, it's another inconsistency.

… or if Podman crashes, or the power goes out, in the meantime. That’s what I meant about “transactional commit semantics”.

Anyway, what motivated you to implement this change? The tests sort of suggest “handling running out of disk space”, but nothing says explicitly. Without that it’s hard to tell what is / isn’t possible to fix, or a good approach.

(I’m also not really familiar with the secrets codebase, and at some point I’ll leave the review to experts — I just wanted to raise that the original version of the PR, as is, doesn’t _reliably_ work.)

---

> Isn't a rename atomic?

Yes, and we rely on that extensively, e.g. via `AtomicWriteFile`.

> I see tests fail, maybe because I used chmod to simulate io errors

I didn’t actually _check_, but I think the tests are running as `root` and thus not constrained by permissions.

Just saw that there are this kinds of edge cases like you told (power goes out, disk went full, etc), can make system inconsistent and wanted to improve things. Anyway, I see no good solution without drastic changes. I will close the PR. Thank you.
